passage.title: The malware family tree
passage.text:
Malware is a broad family with very different members. Viruses attach themselves to legitimate programs and run when those programs run. Worms spread by themselves across networks, exploiting vulnerabilities to move from host to host. Trojans pretend to be useful software but carry malicious payloads. Rootkits hide deep in the operating system, often at kernel level, making detection hard. Spyware quietly collects keystrokes, screenshots and credentials. Ransomware encrypts files and demands payment. Wipers destroy data without any possibility of recovery; they are typically deployed in geopolitical operations rather than for profit.

A security researcher recently said that fileless malware is now one of the fastest-growing categories. Instead of writing files to disk, attackers abuse trusted system tools like PowerShell or Windows Management Instrumentation. Antivirus engines that look for malicious files struggle here, because there are no files to scan. Endpoint Detection and Response platforms have become essential, because they monitor behaviour across the system, not just files.

Attribution is rarely straightforward. Operators reuse code, share infrastructure and sometimes deliberately plant false flags. Mature analysts work with explicit confidence levels: 'low' when the evidence is suggestive but thin, 'high' when multiple independent indicators point in the same direction. The Mandiant report on a recent incident stated that they had observed three distinct toolkits, two of which had been previously linked to a known actor.
passage.source: Adapted from CISA Malware Analysis Reports (cisa.gov, PD), MITRE ATT&CK (attack.mitre.org), and Mandiant M-Trends public summaries.

exercises.q: Which malware type spreads by itself across networks?

exercises.q: Fileless malware abuses trusted tools like ___ or WMI.

exercises.model: Model: Antivirus engines scan files for known patterns. Fileless malware leaves nothing to scan. EDR monitors behaviour across processes and the system, so it detects abuse of legitimate tools.

passage.title: Anatomy of a spear phish
passage.text:
Anna received an email that looked exactly like an internal HR notification. The sender appeared to be 'hr-notifications@hercompany.com'. The subject line said: 'Action required: confirm your bank details for May payroll.' There was a button labelled 'Update details'. She had a moment of doubt and asked her colleague whether the email was legitimate. He suggested hovering over the button. The actual URL was 'https://her-company-payroll-portal.com/login' — not the company domain. It was a spear-phishing attack.

The attackers had clearly done their homework. They knew the company was about to issue an internal communication about payroll changes, because the new HR system had been announced on LinkedIn. They had registered a lookalike domain and built a near-perfect copy of the real login page. Anna's colleague reported it to the security team. The team confirmed it was part of a larger campaign and asked whether anyone in the company had clicked. They had, unfortunately. Three employees had submitted credentials before the email was blocked.

The security team ran the standard playbook. They reset the affected accounts, forced password rotation, reviewed authentication logs, and looked for any subsequent suspicious activity. The chief information security officer asked when the campaign had begun and how the attackers had identified internal communications. The investigation pointed to public LinkedIn posts and a former employee whose access had not been deprovisioned promptly.
passage.source: Adapted from FBI 'Business Email Compromise' advisory (fbi.gov, PD) and CISA spear-phishing guidance (cisa.gov, PD).

grammar.title: Verb + -ing vs verb + to-infinitive
grammar.rule: Some verbs are followed by -ing (avoid, recommend, suggest, mind, enjoy). Others by to-infinitive (decide, plan, want, refuse, agree). A few change meaning depending on the form (stop, remember, try).

head: Pattern
Verbs
Example
rows: She agreed to use a manager.
I stopped to write it (paused) vs I stopped writing it (no longer).

examples.en: I recommend using a password manager.
She decided to enable hardware keys.
I tried changing the rotation policy.
I tried to change the rotation policy.

lesson_notes.tip: Modern NIST guidance: do not enforce periodic rotation unless you suspect compromise. Focus instead on length (≥ 12 characters), checking against breach corpora, and adding a second factor.

passage.title: Modern password management
passage.text:
For decades, organisations enforced passwords that were short, complex and rotated every 90 days. Users responded predictably: they wrote them on sticky notes, varied them by adding a digit at the end, or chose patterns that were easy to remember and easy to break. The 2017 update to NIST Special Publication 800-63B changed the conversation. NIST now recommends focusing on length rather than complexity, banning common and breached passwords, and avoiding mandatory periodic rotation unless there is evidence of compromise.

A password manager is the practical answer for most users. It generates a unique, long password for every site and stores them encrypted under a single master passphrase. Bitwarden, 1Password, and KeePass are well-known options. Security teams sometimes worry that managers create a single point of failure, but the alternative — humans inventing passwords — has been measured many times and the results are consistently worse.

Multi-factor authentication adds a second layer that is not based on what the user knows. Hardware keys based on FIDO2 are now the gold standard, because they are resistant to phishing in a way that SMS or push-notification methods are not. SMS codes can be intercepted through SIM-swap attacks; push fatigue, where attackers spam the user until they accept a prompt, has compromised major companies. The most important habit, however, is checking whether your accounts have already been exposed in known breaches. Free services like Have I Been Pwned make this trivial.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-63B (nist.gov, PD), CISA password guidance (cisa.gov, PD), and the Have I Been Pwned project (haveibeenpwned.com, public service).

exercises.q: Modern NIST guidance focuses primarily on:
NIST recommends mandatory rotation every 90 days.
exercises.explain: 'Avoiding mandatory periodic rotation unless there is evidence of compromise.'

exercises.q: Hardware keys based on ___ are now the gold standard.
Why are SMS codes considered weaker 2FA than hardware keys?
exercises.model: Model: SMS codes can be intercepted via SIM-swap attacks. Hardware keys use cryptographic challenge-response that cannot be replayed by phishers.

lesson_notes.warmup: MFA is the single highest-impact control for credential abuse. Microsoft estimates it blocks more than 99 % of automated account-takeover attempts. The remaining one per cent matters too, which is why the type of MFA matters.

grammar.title: Zero conditional (general truths)
grammar.rule: Form: IF + present simple, present simple. Used for general truths and natural laws — when X happens, Y always happens.

examples.en: If a user enters wrong credentials, the system locks the account.
When MFA is enabled, automated attacks fail.
Unless you use a hardware key, you remain vulnerable to phishing.

mistakes.wrong: If you will enable MFA, attacks fail.
mistakes.right: If you enable MFA, attacks fail.
mistakes.why: Zero conditional → present simple in both clauses; never WILL in the IF.

mistakes.wrong: Unless you don't use MFA, you are safer.
mistakes.right: Unless you use MFA, you are vulnerable.
mistakes.why: UNLESS already negative; do not double-negate.

lesson_notes.tip: Avoid SMS as your primary MFA. SIM-swap attacks have compromised cryptocurrency exchanges, telecom executives, and even SS7 protocols. Use authenticator apps or, better, FIDO2 hardware keys.

passage.title: Choosing your MFA factor
passage.text:
Multi-factor authentication combines two or more independent factors: something you know (a password), something you have (a phone or a hardware key), and something you are (a fingerprint, a face). When all three are required, attacks become considerably harder. Yet not all MFA factors are equal, and confusing them is a common mistake.

SMS codes were the original second factor. They are simple, but they have well-documented weaknesses. If an attacker convinces a mobile carrier to transfer the victim's number to a new SIM (a SIM-swap attack), the codes go to the attacker. Push notifications, used by apps like Microsoft Authenticator and Duo, are a small step up. They tie the second factor to a specific device. However, push fatigue attacks have shown that users will accept prompts they did not initiate if they are bombarded enough times.

Time-based one-time passwords, or TOTP, are stronger. Apps generate codes locally without needing the internet, which removes the SMS-interception risk. The user still has to copy the code, which makes the method phishable: if the user enters their TOTP into a fake site, the attacker can use it within the 30-second window.

Hardware keys based on the FIDO2 / WebAuthn standard are the strongest mainstream option. They sign a cryptographic challenge tied to the actual website domain. If the domain is wrong, the key refuses to authenticate. This makes them genuinely phishing-resistant. The latest evolution, passkeys, brings the same protection to consumer accounts without requiring a separate hardware device.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-63B (nist.gov, PD), FIDO Alliance public specifications (fidoalliance.org), and Microsoft Identity blog (microsoft.com/security).

exercises.q: Which factor is described as 'genuinely phishing-resistant'?
exercises.explain: 'Hardware keys based on the FIDO2/WebAuthn standard… genuinely phishing-resistant.'

exercises.q: TOTP codes are immune to phishing.
exercises.explain: 'Phishable: if the user enters their TOTP into a fake site…'

exercises.q: A SIM-swap attack convinces a mobile carrier to transfer the victim's ___ .
Why does FIDO2 prevent phishing in a way TOTP cannot?
exercises.model: Model: FIDO2 binds the cryptographic response to the actual domain. If the user is on a fake site, the key refuses to authenticate. TOTP codes have no such binding and can be replayed by an attacker.

lesson_notes.tip: Two rules of crypto in practice: never invent your own primitives, never disable certificate validation. Both are responsible for a disproportionate share of real incidents.

passage.title: What encryption actually does
passage.text:
Encryption transforms readable data, called plaintext, into something unintelligible without the key. The two main families are symmetric and asymmetric. Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption. AES is the standard symmetric algorithm; AES-256 in GCM mode is the modern recommendation. Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair: a public key, which anyone can have, and a private key, which only the owner has. RSA was the dominant asymmetric algorithm for decades, but it is increasingly being replaced by elliptic curve cryptography for performance reasons.

Most real systems combine both. TLS, the protocol that protects every secure web connection, uses asymmetric cryptography to securely exchange a session key, and then symmetric AES to encrypt the actual traffic. This hybrid approach gives the security of asymmetric and the speed of symmetric.

Security does not come from the algorithm alone. Implementation matters enormously. The OpenSSL Heartbleed bug in 2014 had nothing to do with broken AES; it was a buffer over-read that leaked private keys. Padding oracle attacks, timing attacks and side-channel attacks have repeatedly broken otherwise sound algorithms. Modern AEAD modes — Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data — close many of these gaps by binding integrity to confidentiality.

Looking forward, the cryptographic community is preparing for the post-quantum era. NIST has standardised ML-KEM (formerly Kyber) for key encapsulation and ML-DSA for digital signatures. Major browsers and cloud providers are going to deploy hybrid post-quantum TLS in 2026, combining a classical and a post-quantum primitive for safety.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST FIPS 197 / 203 / 204 (nist.gov, PD), IETF RFC 8446 TLS 1.3 (rfc-editor.org), and Cloudflare research blog (CC BY).

exercises.q: Which encryption type uses the same key for encryption and decryption?
exercises.explain: 'Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both…'

exercises.q: Heartbleed was caused by a broken AES implementation.
exercises.explain: 'Heartbleed bug had nothing to do with broken AES.'

exercises.q: NIST has standardised ML-KEM (formerly ___).
Why are AEAD modes important for modern encryption?
exercises.model: Model: They bind authenticity (integrity) to confidentiality, preventing classic attacks like padding oracles. Without authentication, an attacker can sometimes manipulate ciphertext to learn plaintext.

lesson_notes.tip: Antivirus is now a commodity. Endpoint Detection and Response is the new floor for any serious enterprise. EDR sees behaviour, not just files, and ships with telemetry that fuels detection engineering.

passage.title: From firewalls to EDR
passage.text:
For two decades, the perimeter firewall was the cornerstone of corporate security. The firewall, which inspected traffic at the network boundary, blocked unauthorised connections based on rules about ports and addresses. As applications moved to the cloud and employees moved to laptops, this model lost much of its relevance. The boundary that the firewall protected is now diffuse: data lives in many cloud providers and devices live everywhere.

Next-generation firewalls added application awareness and TLS inspection. Web Application Firewalls, which sit in front of web applications, look at HTTP traffic for injection patterns. Both still matter, but the bigger story is at the endpoint.

Antivirus, which was the consumer-grade endpoint control for decades, was based on signatures: known patterns of known threats. The model worked when malware was rare and slow-changing. Today's threats are polymorphic, fileless and often built specifically for the target. Endpoint Detection and Response, or EDR, replaced AV as the enterprise floor. EDR continuously records process execution, registry changes, network connections and file activity. Detection rules look for patterns that suggest malicious behaviour, even when no specific malware is known.

Extended Detection and Response (XDR) is the natural next step. XDR, which integrates endpoint, network, identity and cloud telemetry, gives analysts a unified view of an incident. The challenge is no longer collecting data; it is sifting through too much of it. The most valuable engineers in modern security operations are those who write detections that fire on real attacks without flooding the analyst with noise.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-41 (nist.gov, PD), Gartner public research summaries on EDR/XDR, and OWASP CRS public docs (CC BY-SA).

exercises.q: What does WAF stand for?
AV is now considered the floor for enterprise endpoint security.
exercises.explain: 'EDR replaced AV as the enterprise floor.'

exercises.q: ___ integrates endpoint, network, identity and cloud telemetry.
Why has signature-based AV become insufficient?
exercises.model: Model: Modern threats are polymorphic, fileless, and often built specifically for the target. Signatures only catch known patterns; they miss novel and behaviour-only attacks.

lesson_notes.warmup: Ethical hacking is offensive security with permission. The skills are the same as criminal hacking; the legal authorisation, scope and intent are different. Confusing the two has ended careers.

grammar.title: Present perfect vs past simple
grammar.rule: Present perfect = past event with present relevance, recent or unspecified time, life experience. Past simple = finished event at a specific past time. Time markers determine the choice.

lesson_notes.tip: Always have a written authorisation. Without it, the same scan that earns you a bonus on Tuesday earns you a court date on Wednesday.

passage.title: Inside an ethical hacking engagement
passage.text:
Ethical hackers, also called penetration testers or red teamers, simulate the actions of real attackers to find weaknesses before criminals do. Their work is structured by the rules of engagement: a written agreement that defines what is in scope, what is off-limits, and what to do if testing accidentally affects production systems.

A typical engagement begins with reconnaissance. The tester gathers information from public sources — DNS records, LinkedIn profiles, code repositories — building a picture of the target's attack surface. Active scanning follows: the tester probes services, fingerprints software versions, and looks for known vulnerabilities. Then comes exploitation: the tester attempts to gain access in controlled ways, escalates privileges where possible, and tries to move laterally to demonstrate the realistic impact of a compromise. Finally, post-exploitation activities document evidence and prepare for clean-up.

The report is the deliverable that customers actually pay for. A good report does not just list findings; it explains business impact, recommends prioritised remediation, and provides reproduction steps so that defenders can verify the fix. The best testers I have worked with have always treated the writing as the most important part of the engagement.

The legal context matters enormously. The US Department of Justice updated its CFAA charging policy in 2022 to abjure prosecution of good-faith security research. The UK Computer Misuse Act of 1990 has not been comparably reformed, despite a decade of campaigning by the CyberUp coalition. Researchers operating internationally have to navigate this patchwork carefully.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-115 Technical Guide to Information Security Testing (nist.gov, PD), OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (owasp.org, CC BY-SA), and US DoJ CFAA Charging Policy 2022 (justice.gov, PD).

exercises.q: What document defines what is in scope and off-limits?
exercises.explain: 'Rules of engagement: a written agreement that defines…'

exercises.q: The UK Computer Misuse Act has been reformed for ethical research.
The most valuable part of the engagement, according to the author, is the ___ .
Why is written authorisation essential before testing?
exercises.model: Model: Without written authorisation, the same activity is unauthorised access, which is criminal in most jurisdictions. Authorisation defines scope, protects the tester legally, and clarifies what to do if production is affected.

passage.title: The Internet of Things and its risks
passage.text:
The Internet of Things describes the explosion of connected devices that surround modern life: smart bulbs, doorbell cameras, fitness trackers, industrial sensors, medical implants. Each adds value, and each adds risk. Many of these devices ship with default passwords, never receive firmware updates, and run software stacks that the original vendor stopped maintaining years ago.

The Mirai botnet of 2016 was a watershed moment. Mirai infected hundreds of thousands of cheap routers and IP cameras by trying a short list of default credentials. The infected devices were used to launch distributed denial-of-service attacks of unprecedented scale, taking down major DNS providers and household-name websites for hours. The most striking thing about Mirai was its simplicity: no zero-days, no clever exploits, just default passwords nobody had changed.

In industrial environments, the stakes are higher. Operational Technology controls power grids, water treatment plants, oil pipelines and factory floors. Stuxnet showed in 2010 what targeted OT malware can achieve. Recent incidents at water utilities and a US fuel pipeline have shown that less sophisticated attackers can also cause serious disruption when industrial controllers are connected to the internet without segmentation.

If consumer IoT manufacturers prioritised security as much as time-to-market, the situation would be very different. Regulators are slowly forcing change. The EU Cyber Resilience Act, the US IoT Cybersecurity Improvement Act, and the UK Product Security and Telecommunications Infrastructure Act all impose minimum requirements. Compliance is costly. Devices without it will be banned from the markets that adopt these laws.
passage.source: Adapted from CISA OT and IoT advisories (cisa.gov, PD), the Mirai botnet analysis published in academic literature, and ENISA reports on IoT security (enisa.europa.eu).

exercises.q: How did Mirai infect devices?
exercises.explain: 'Trying a short list of default credentials.'

exercises.q: Stuxnet targeted consumer IoT devices.
exercises.explain: 'Stuxnet showed… what targeted OT malware can achieve.'

exercises.q: The EU regulation on connected products is the Cyber ___ Act.
Why is consumer IoT a recurring problem in corporate networks?
exercises.model: Model: Consumer-grade devices ship with weak defaults, rarely receive updates, and are often connected without segmentation. They become persistent footholds and are hard to detect because they are not in any inventory.

mistakes.right: As long as you verify, you are safe.

lesson_notes.tip: Treat LLM outputs like user input: untrusted, fallible, and capable of triggering downstream actions. Prompt injection is the SQL injection of our era.

passage.title: What machine learning actually is
passage.text:
Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence in which a computer learns patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed. Within ML, three main paradigms coexist. Supervised learning trains on labelled data: classification of email as spam or not, prediction of customer churn. Unsupervised learning finds structure without labels: clustering, anomaly detection. Reinforcement learning rewards an agent for achieving objectives in an environment.

Deep learning is a particular family within ML based on neural networks with many layers. The neural network architecture has not changed fundamentally since the 1980s, but the combination of huge datasets, GPUs, and clever training techniques has produced systems that perform astonishingly well on tasks like image recognition and natural-language processing.

Large Language Models, or LLMs, are the recent wave that has reached the mainstream. They are trained on enormous text corpora to predict the next token. ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini are LLMs accessed through chat interfaces. Their strengths are surprising; their failure modes are also surprising. They confidently generate plausible but false information ('hallucination'), they can be manipulated by adversarial prompts ('prompt injection'), and they reflect biases present in their training data.

As long as engineers understand these failure modes, LLMs are extraordinarily useful. Unless they understand them, deploying LLMs into critical decisions is dangerous. Retrieval-Augmented Generation grounds the model in trusted documents, which mitigates hallucination but introduces new risks if the document corpus is poisoned. The OWASP Top 10 for LLMs is a good starting point for any team building production AI systems.
passage.source: Adapted from MIT 6.S191 'Introduction to Deep Learning' (introtodeeplearning.com, CC BY-NC-SA), OWASP Top 10 for LLMs (owasp.org, CC BY-SA) and Anthropic / Stanford HAI public materials.

exercises.q: Which paradigm trains on labelled data?
Hallucination is when an LLM refuses to answer.
exercises.explain: 'They confidently generate plausible but false information.'

exercises.q: ___ Generation grounds the model in trusted documents.
Why is RAG not a complete solution to hallucination?
exercises.model: Model: RAG grounds answers in retrieved documents, but if the corpus is incomplete or poisoned, the model still produces incorrect outputs. RAG reduces hallucination but does not eliminate it.

grammar.title: Third conditional (introduction)
grammar.rule: Form: IF + past perfect, WOULD HAVE + past participle. Talks about how the past would have differed if a condition had been met. Counterfactual reasoning.

head: Form
Example
rows: IF + past perfect, would have + p.p.
If they had audited the contract, the hack would not have happened.
If they had used MFA, the keys wouldn't have been stolen.
If we had bought BTC in 2010, we could have retired.

examples.en: If The DAO had had timelocks, the attacker would not have succeeded.
If the protocol had been more decentralised, governance would have been harder to capture.

phrases.phrase: The use case is questionable because …

mistakes.wrong: If they audited it, the hack wouldn't happen.
mistakes.right: If they had audited it, the hack wouldn't have happened.
mistakes.why: Past counterfactual → past perfect + would have p.p.

mistakes.wrong: If they would have used MFA, keys safe.
mistakes.right: If they had used MFA, keys would have been safe.
mistakes.why: IF clause → past perfect; never WOULD HAVE.

lesson_notes.tip: Most 'blockchain' projects could be a database. The genuine use cases — censorship-resistant value transfer, public-state transparency in adversarial environments — are narrower than the marketing suggests.

passage.title: Blockchain in plain English
passage.text:
A blockchain is a distributed ledger maintained by many independent participants. Each block contains transactions, a timestamp, and a cryptographic hash of the previous block. Changing any historical block would invalidate every block after it, which makes the chain tamper-evident. Bitcoin, launched in 2009 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto, was the first widely successful implementation. Ethereum, launched in 2015, generalised the model by adding a built-in programming language for smart contracts.

Consensus is the hard part. Bitcoin uses Proof of Work: participants compete to solve cryptographic puzzles, and the winner adds the next block. The model is robust but consumes vast amounts of electricity. Proof of Stake replaces computational work with economic stake: validators lock up tokens and risk losing them if they misbehave. Ethereum migrated to Proof of Stake in 2022, reducing its energy consumption by more than 99%.

Smart contracts are programs that run on the blockchain. They enable Decentralised Finance (DeFi), Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs), and Decentralised Autonomous Organisations (DAOs). Their power is also their risk: smart contracts often hold significant value and are immutable once deployed. The 2016 DAO hack, in which an attacker drained roughly 60 million dollars worth of ETH through a re-entrancy bug, would not have happened if the team had subjected the contract to formal verification. Repeated DeFi exploits since then have followed similar patterns.

For a systems engineer, the practical question is whether a given problem actually needs decentralisation. If the answer is no, blockchain adds complexity without proportional benefit.
passage.source: Adapted from Bitcoin and Ethereum white papers (bitcoin.org, ethereum.org, public), and DeFi exploit analyses from Rekt News public reports.

exercises.q: What does Ethereum's 2022 transition reduce?
exercises.explain: 'Reducing its energy consumption by more than 99%.'

exercises.q: Smart contracts can be edited after deployment.
The DAO hack drained approximately ___ million dollars.
When does blockchain genuinely add value over a database?
exercises.model: Model: When trust is genuinely distributed and adversarial — for example, censorship-resistant value transfer or public-state transparency where no single party can be trusted to maintain the ledger.

head: Form
Use
Example

lesson_notes.tip: Estimate in story points, not hours. Hours invite false precision. Points capture relative complexity, which is what teams actually have intuition about.

passage.title: Agile in the real world
passage.text:
Most modern software teams describe themselves as agile, but the practice varies enormously. The Agile Manifesto of 2001 emphasised individuals and interactions over processes, working software over comprehensive documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, and responding to change over following a plan. Two decades later, those values are widely accepted; their implementation is the contested part.

Scrum is the most common framework. A Scrum team has three roles: a Product Owner who prioritises the backlog, a Scrum Master who facilitates the process, and developers who deliver the work. Sprints are typically two weeks long. Each sprint begins with planning, runs through daily stand-ups, and ends with a review of the increment and a retrospective on the process.

Kanban, by contrast, has no fixed iterations. Work flows continuously through stages — backlog, in progress, in review, done — with limits on work in progress that prevent overload. Kanban suits operational work where priorities change frequently. Many real teams use a hybrid: Scrum for new development, Kanban for support and bug fixes.

Common failure patterns are well documented. Sprints become endurance tests when scope expands during the sprint. Stand-ups become status reports rather than coordination meetings. Retrospectives become rituals where teams complain about the same problems for months. The classic post-mortem comment — 'we should have raised this risk earlier' — appears so often that wise teams turn it into a checklist item. Effective project management is not about following a framework rigidly; it is about building feedback loops that surface problems while they are still cheap to fix.
passage.source: Adapted from the Scrum Guide (scrumguides.org, CC BY-SA), the Agile Manifesto (agilemanifesto.org, public), and the Kanban method public material.

exercises.q: Which framework has no fixed iterations?
exercises.explain: 'Kanban, by contrast, has no fixed iterations.'

exercises.q: The Agile Manifesto states processes over individuals.
A Scrum sprint is typically ___ weeks long.
Why do retrospectives sometimes lose their value?
exercises.model: Model: When teams complain about the same problems month after month without acting on them, retrospectives become rituals rather than improvement loops. Effective retros translate complaints into specific actions with owners and timelines.

grammar.title: Linking words: HOWEVER, MOREOVER, THEREFORE, NEVERTHELESS
grammar.rule: Use sentence-level connectors followed by a comma to make professional writing flow. They go at the start of a new sentence, not joining two clauses.

head: Linker
Function
Example
rows: The patch is critical. However, the maintenance window is full.
The system is slow. Moreover, the logs are corrupted.
The disk is full. Therefore, backups are failing.

mistakes.wrong: Moreover of that, the logs are corrupted.

lesson_notes.tip: In a professional email, lead with the ask. The first sentence should make clear what you need from the reader. The rest is justification.

passage.title: A professional escalation email
passage.text:
Subject: Action required: vulnerability CVE-2026-12345 in production database

Dear Mark,

Following our security review on Tuesday, I am writing to escalate a finding that I consider time-sensitive. Our production database server is running version 14.2.0 of PostgreSQL, which is affected by CVE-2026-12345. The vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote code execution. The patched version, 14.2.1, has been available since last week.

We should patch by this Friday at the latest. However, the standard maintenance window is fully booked until next month. I would propose we treat this as an emergency change. Moreover, similar CVEs have already been observed in the wild within forty-eight hours of disclosure. Therefore, the risk of waiting is, in my judgement, unacceptable.

I have attached a draft change request and a rollback plan. Could you please confirm whether you can sponsor this as an emergency change? I would appreciate your confirmation by 17:00 today, so that we can coordinate with the database team and execute over the weekend if approved.

Nevertheless, if you have a different reading of the risk, I am very open to discussing it. I am available for a quick call any time before 16:00.

Thank you for your time.

Best regards,
Laura Mendoza
Senior Systems Engineer
passage.source: Original. Email register and CVE handling consistent with NIST SP 800-40 patch management guidance (nist.gov, PD).

exercises.q: What is Laura asking Mark to do?
Similar CVEs are usually exploited within a year.
The version that fixes the issue is 14.2.___.
Why does Laura include 'I am very open to discussing it'?
exercises.model: Model: To soften the escalation tone and signal that she respects Mark's authority and judgement, while still pushing for urgent action. It is a register move, not a retreat.

Example

lesson_notes.tip: Rule of three: structure your talk around three main points. Audiences remember three. Two feels thin. Five feels exhausting.

passage.title: Anatomy of a 5-minute talk
passage.text:
Good morning everyone, and thank you for joining. I'm Carlos Mendez, senior systems engineer in the platform team. Today I'd like to talk about our cloud migration. I'll cover three things: where we are, what we have learned, and what we propose for next quarter.

First, where we are. We started the migration in January. As of today, we have moved sixty per cent of services from our on-premise data centre to AWS. The remaining services are mostly databases and integrations with legacy systems.

Moving on, what we have learned. Two things stand out. The first is that the lift-and-shift approach is faster but produces higher running costs. We are paying more on AWS than we were on-premise for some workloads, simply because we did not redesign them. The second is that observability matters. Without good monitoring, you do not know whether the new environment is working until users complain.

Finally, what we propose for next quarter. We want to slow down the lift-and-shift, and start a parallel track of refactoring the most expensive workloads. This will take more engineering time but will reduce running cost by an estimated thirty per cent.

In summary: sixty per cent migrated, two key lessons, and a proposal to refactor instead of rushing. I am happy to take questions. Thank you.
passage.source: Original. Talk structure inspired by 'Talk Like TED' (Carmine Gallo) and IEEE professional communication guidance.

exercises.model: Model: 1) Lift-and-shift is faster but produces higher running costs because workloads are not redesigned. 2) Observability matters; without monitoring, problems only surface when users complain.

passage.title: Ready for B1
passage.text:
My name is Daniel and I have been studying English for two years. When I started, I could only describe simple things. Now I can read tech articles, understand video calls in English, and write professional emails to colleagues abroad. I have also passed two practice PET exams with scores around 145 on the Cambridge scale, which is a B1 borderline B2.

In the last six months, I have focused on Use of English. The transformations were hard at first. If I had not done the daily exercises, I would still be struggling with passive forms and reported speech. The reading is now my strongest skill. I read at least one IEEE Spectrum article a week, and I take notes on five new words each time.

The weakest area is still listening. Native speakers talk fast, especially in podcasts. I now listen to VOA Learning English at full speed and to BBC Radio Tech twice a week. The first listen is for general meaning, the second for details. I have noticed that my comprehension has jumped in the past month.

The exam is in three weeks. My plan is: one practice paper every Saturday, vocabulary review every morning, and one IT podcast every evening. If I keep this routine, I will pass. After PET, I want to start B2 First. My goal is C1 in two years and a senior position abroad in three.
passage.source: Original. PET exam structure from Cambridge Assessment English handbook (cambridgeenglish.org).

passage.title: Cybersecurity incident response
passage.text:
When a cyber incident occurs, the difference between a minor disruption and a catastrophic breach is almost always the quality of the response. The National Institute of Standards and Technology, NIST, published Special Publication 800-61 Revision 2, which has become the de facto reference across the industry. It describes a four-phase lifecycle: Preparation, Detection and Analysis, Containment Eradication and Recovery, and Post-Incident Activity. Each phase is critical, and skipping any of them tends to have serious consequences later.

Preparation is the phase that organisations often underestimate. It includes writing playbooks, building the CSIRT or Computer Security Incident Response Team, practising tabletop exercises and making sure that basic logging is enabled across every endpoint. A mature programme also includes retainer agreements with external forensic firms, because a small team rarely has the capacity to investigate a large-scale compromise alone. When an alert arrives, analysts move to Detection and Analysis. They triage the event, assess its severity, build an initial timeline, and try to identify indicators of compromise. Modern SOCs rely heavily on SIEM platforms, EDR agents and threat intelligence feeds during this phase.

Containment can be short-term or long-term. Short-term containment means isolating the affected host immediately, for example by cutting its network connection. Long-term containment includes rebuilding systems from clean images and rotating credentials. Eradication removes the attacker's tools, persistence mechanisms and backdoors, while Recovery brings systems back to normal operation with enhanced monitoring for at least 30 days.

The final phase, Post-Incident Activity, is where learning happens. A blameless retrospective identifies what worked, what failed, and which controls need to change. The 2020 SolarWinds compromise, which affected thousands of organisations including several US federal agencies, is a textbook case study: it demonstrated the limits of perimeter thinking and accelerated adoption of Zero Trust architectures worldwide. It also showed how deeply attackers can hide in the software supply chain, a risk that many organisations had seriously underestimated until then.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-61 Rev 2 (nist.gov, PD) and CISA SolarWinds analysis (cisa.gov, PD).

exercises.q: Which NIST document defines the incident response lifecycle?
Post-incident activity is skipped when the incident is contained.
Isolating an affected host by cutting its network connection is an example of short-term ____.
Why did SolarWinds accelerate Zero Trust adoption?
exercises.model: Model: Because attackers moved laterally after entering through a trusted update, showing that perimeter-based trust assumptions were no longer safe.

use_of_english.explain: 'Often' fits naturally; the others contradict the passage.

use_of_english.sentence: A mature programme also includes retainer agreements ___ external forensic firms.
Short-term ___ means isolating the affected host immediately.

use_of_english.gap_template: The Preparation phase ___ without serious later consequences.
use_of_english.explain: Modal passive: cannot be + past participle.

SolarWinds is described ___ a textbook case.

use_of_english.original: It is critical to prepare in advance.
The team must learn from every incident.

lesson_notes.warmup: The difference between a minor incident and a catastrophic breach is almost never the technology. It is the preparation. A team that has rehearsed its playbooks and knows its escalation paths responds in minutes, not hours.

grammar.title: Mixed conditionals + third conditional
grammar.rule: Third conditional: If + past perfect, would have + past participle. Describes imaginary past situations. Mixed conditional combines past condition with present result.

head: Type
Structure
Use
rows: If + past perfect, would have + p.p.
If we had segmented the network, the breach wouldn't have spread.
If we had hired a CISO earlier, we wouldn't be in this situation.
If + past simple, would have + p.p.
If the team were better trained, they would have contained it quickly.

examples.en: If SolarWinds had applied stronger SDLC controls, the compromise would have been harder.
If we weren't so dependent on a single vendor, we wouldn't have been paralysed.

phrases.phrase: This could have been avoided by …

mistakes.wrong: If we would have segmented, the breach wouldn't happen.
mistakes.right: If we had segmented, the breach wouldn't have spread.
mistakes.why: In the IF clause of 3rd conditional → past perfect, not 'would have'.

mistakes.wrong: If the team trained, they had contained it.
mistakes.right: If the team had been trained, they would have contained it.
mistakes.why: 3rd conditional needs past perfect + would have.

lesson_notes.tip: In post-incident reports, use conditionals with care. They imply blame. A neutral phrase like 'A stronger control would have prevented this' is safer than 'If you had configured X correctly'.

lessons.vocab_raw: +70: defense in depth, perimeter, zone, DMZ, segment, microsegment, east-west, north-south, least privilege, zero trust, SD-WAN, SASE, ZTNA, CASB, SWG, proxy forward, reverse, IDS, IPS, NDR, TAP, SPAN, flow, NetFlow, sFlow, IPFIX, packet capture, Wireshark, pcap, deep packet inspection, TLS inspection, MITM, PKI internal, certificate pinning

passage.title: Network security architecture
passage.text:
Modern enterprise networks look nothing like the flat, trusted LANs of the 1990s. Today, segmentation, defence in depth and strong identity controls are considered baseline requirements, not optional extras. A well-designed architecture combines perimeter controls, micro-segmentation inside the network, and rigorous east-west inspection between workloads.

At the perimeter, next-generation firewalls, or NGFWs, inspect not only IP addresses and ports, but also application protocols and TLS metadata. Web Application Firewalls, or WAFs, sit in front of web apps and block common attacks like SQL injection and cross-site scripting. Intrusion detection and prevention systems generate alerts for suspicious patterns and, in prevention mode, actively drop malicious packets. DMZs – demilitarised zones – isolate public-facing servers from the internal network, so that a compromise of the web server does not immediately reach the databases.

Inside the network, micro-segmentation goes several steps further than traditional VLAN-based segmentation. Each workload is placed in a tiny zone, and only strictly necessary flows are allowed. If an attacker compromises one server, they cannot easily pivot to another. Approaches such as SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) combine network security and SD-WAN into a single cloud-delivered service, while ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) replaces traditional VPNs with per-application, identity-aware access.

TLS inspection deserves a special mention. Because more than 95% of enterprise traffic is now encrypted, blind spots have grown. Many organisations terminate TLS at a security appliance to inspect payloads for malware before re-encrypting the traffic. This practice, however, creates its own privacy, performance and compliance challenges, and it must be balanced carefully against employee rights and regulatory constraints. A good reference architecture, therefore, is never only technology – it is the result of a continuous conversation between security engineers, legal teams, privacy officers and the business.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture (nist.gov, PD) and ENISA 'Guidelines for Securing the IoT' (enisa.europa.eu).

exercises.q: Which of these replaces traditional VPNs with per-application, identity-aware access?
exercises.explain: 'ZTNA… replaces traditional VPNs with per-application, identity-aware access.'

passage.title: Ransomware and double extortion
passage.text:
Ransomware is no longer the amateur phenomenon it was a decade ago. The ecosystem has matured into a professional criminal industry, often organised as Ransomware-as-a-Service, or RaaS. In this model, a core developer group builds and maintains the malware, while affiliates conduct the actual intrusions and split the ransom, typically 70–80% for the affiliate and the rest for the core operators. Initial access is frequently bought from specialised brokers who sell corporate credentials or ready-to-use footholds on underground markets.

Since around 2019, almost every major operator has moved to double extortion. Before encrypting files, attackers exfiltrate gigabytes or even terabytes of sensitive data. If the victim refuses to pay, the criminals publish the data on a leak site. Some groups, such as LockBit and ALPHV, have gone further and added triple extortion, launching DDoS attacks or contacting the victim's customers directly. For public companies, the reputational damage can exceed the operational impact of the encryption itself.

The 2021 attack on Colonial Pipeline is a textbook case. A single compromised VPN credential gave the DarkSide group access to the internal network. Fearing lateral movement into operational technology, Colonial shut down the pipeline for six days, triggering fuel shortages across the Eastern United States. The company paid roughly 4.4 million US dollars in Bitcoin, and although the FBI later recovered most of it, the lesson was clear: modern organisations can no longer treat ransomware as a purely technical problem.

Paying or not paying is a complex decision involving legal, ethical and operational dimensions. In some jurisdictions, payments to sanctioned groups are now illegal. Insurance companies are increasingly reluctant to reimburse ransoms. The only durable defence is a combination of strong identity controls, immutable backups, effective detection, and rehearsed response playbooks. Tested backups, in particular, are the single most important control: if data can be restored quickly from a clean copy, attackers lose most of their leverage, whatever encryption they have deployed.
passage.source: Adapted from CISA StopRansomware advisories (cisa.gov, PD) and ENISA Threat Landscape 2024 (enisa.europa.eu).

passage.title: Zero-day vulnerabilities
passage.text:
A zero-day vulnerability is a flaw that is unknown to the vendor and for which no official patch exists at the moment of discovery. The term comes from the fact that defenders have had 'zero days' to prepare. Once a vulnerability is patched, it becomes what the industry calls an n-day. N-days are still dangerous, because many organisations take weeks or months to apply critical updates, but they are qualitatively different from true zero-days, which give attackers a significant window of advantage.

Zero-days are discovered by a wide range of actors. Academic researchers, independent bug hunters and professional teams like Google Project Zero publish coordinated disclosures after working with vendors to release a fix. Others operate in the grey market: brokers such as Zerodium buy exploits and resell them, mostly to government customers. At competitions like Pwn2Own, researchers demonstrate live exploits against fully patched systems for cash prizes, and the chains of vulnerabilities they build can be remarkably complex, sometimes combining four or five flaws.

The severity of a zero-day depends on what it allows. Remote code execution without authentication on a widely deployed product – the infamous Log4Shell of 2021 is the canonical example – is the most dangerous category. Privilege escalation, sandbox escape and information leaks are also valuable, especially when they chain with other bugs. The EPSS score, or Exploit Prediction Scoring System, helps defenders to prioritise vulnerabilities by predicting the probability of exploitation in the wild within 30 days, complementing the traditional CVSS severity rating.

For defenders, the harsh truth is that a zero-day cannot be patched before public disclosure. The only effective strategy is defence in depth: network segmentation, endpoint detection, strict application allow-listing, rigorous logging, and mature incident response. When a zero-day breaks the news, mature organisations already know which systems run the vulnerable software, where they sit in the network, and how fast they can be patched or isolated. Those that only start asking those questions after the news drops are usually the ones that end up in the next breach report.
passage.source: Adapted from CISA KEV catalogue (cisa.gov, PD), Google Project Zero public blog (googleprojectzero.blogspot.com) and EPSS documentation (first.org, CC BY 4.0).

passage.title: OWASP Top 10 web application risks
passage.text:
The OWASP Top 10 is the most widely cited document in application security. It is a ranked list of the most critical risks to modern web applications, compiled from real-world data contributed by dozens of organisations. It is updated roughly every three to four years, and the 2021 edition – still widely used alongside the 2025 draft – reshuffled the rankings in important ways.

The number one risk in 2021 is Broken Access Control: applications that fail to properly enforce which users can access which resources. Classic examples include IDOR bugs, where simply changing an ID in the URL lets one user read another user's data, and missing function-level authorisation checks. Cryptographic Failures, previously called 'Sensitive Data Exposure', takes second place and covers everything from missing TLS to weak hashing algorithms and hard-coded secrets committed to Git.

Injection, which sat at the top for more than a decade, dropped to third. It still includes the classic SQL injection but now also covers NoSQL, command, LDAP and template injection. The root cause in almost every case is the same: the application concatenates untrusted input into a query or command instead of using parameterised APIs. Insecure Design, a new category, reflects a growing recognition that security problems often start long before any code is written: a flawed architecture or a missing threat model cannot be patched by clever input sanitisation.

Other categories include Security Misconfiguration, Vulnerable and Outdated Components, Authentication Failures, Software and Data Integrity Failures, Security Logging and Monitoring Failures, and finally Server-Side Request Forgery, which was promoted into the Top 10 after several high-profile incidents including the 2019 Capital One breach. For a systems engineer, the practical message is simple: every category is preventable with well-known techniques, but only when security is designed in from the beginning, validated through testing, and verified continuously in production. Reading and genuinely understanding the OWASP Top 10 is no longer optional – it is a professional baseline.
passage.source: Adapted from OWASP Top 10 – 2021 (owasp.org, CC BY-SA 4.0) and 2025 release candidate.

passage.title: Modes, padding and the wiring of crypto
passage.text:
Cryptography in production is rarely about choosing an algorithm. It is about wiring it correctly. Engineers facing real systems must combine primitives, modes, padding, and key management decisions, each of which can break the others.

Consider AES, the standard symmetric cipher since 2001. AES itself has not been broken. Modes built on AES, however, have repeatedly been the source of incidents. ECB mode, exposing patterns in plaintext, is widely considered unsafe and is still observed in legacy systems. CBC mode, vulnerable to padding-oracle attacks when not implemented carefully, has been retired from TLS 1.3 entirely. The current default is GCM, which combines AES with Galois-mode authentication, providing confidentiality and integrity in a single primitive.

Key management remains the part that fails most often in practice. Keys hardcoded in source code, leaked through stack traces, or stored in environment variables visible to every process on a host appear in a depressing share of incidents. Modern guidance favours key managers — AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault — that hold the actual keys and expose only operations.

The random number generator deserves special mention. Cryptographic primitives assume a true source of unpredictability for nonces, IVs, and key material. Generators seeded predictably, broken in low-entropy boot environments, or bypassed for performance have produced repeated catastrophic failures. The 2008 Debian OpenSSL bug, having reduced the entropy pool to 32,000 possible values, made all keys generated on affected systems trivially recoverable.

The consistent lesson, drawn from decades of incidents, is that secure cryptography is not the result of clever choices in isolation; it is the result of restraint, defaults, and code review.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-38D (GCM, nist.gov, PD), TLS 1.3 RFC 8446 (rfc-editor.org), and the Debian DSA-1571 OpenSSL advisory (public).

exercises.model: Model: Reduced entropy made keys predictable. The cryptographic primitive was sound, but the source of randomness was broken, which collapsed the security of every key generated on the affected systems.

passage.title: How PKI actually works
passage.text:
Public Key Infrastructure binds public keys to verified identities through digital certificates. The model rests on a chain of trust: a root certificate, trusted by the operating system or browser, signs intermediate certificates, which in turn sign the end-entity certificates that protect web sites and other services.

The issuance of a certificate is a procedural matter. The applicant generates a key pair, submits a certificate signing request, and proves control of the domain — typically by serving a token at a specific URL or by adding a DNS record. Automation, particularly through the ACME protocol popularised by Let's Encrypt, has reduced this process from days of human work to seconds of machine work. As a result, more than 95% of public web traffic is now encrypted.

Revocation is harder. When a private key is compromised, the certificate must be marked invalid before its expiry. Certificate Revocation Lists, downloaded periodically by clients, were the original mechanism but scale poorly. The Online Certificate Status Protocol replaced CRLs for many use cases. OCSP stapling, in which the server attaches a fresh signed status to its handshake, removed both the privacy and the performance objections to OCSP and is now the de facto standard.

The weakest link of PKI remains the issuance of a fraudulent certificate by a misconfigured or compromised CA. Certificate Transparency, which requires every issued certificate to be logged in publicly auditable logs, has dramatically improved the detection of such issuances. Site operators monitor the logs for unauthorised certificates issued for their domains. The reduction in mis-issuance incidents over the last decade is largely attributable to this transparency, not to changes in CA practice.
passage.source: Adapted from RFC 5280 (X.509, rfc-editor.org), Let's Encrypt public docs (letsencrypt.org), and Certificate Transparency RFC 6962.

passage.title: From VPN to ZTNA
passage.text:
For two decades the VPN was the default mechanism for remote access. The model was simple: an authenticated tunnel from the user's device to the corporate network, after which the user effectively sat 'inside' the perimeter. The technology worked when the network was the trust boundary and most resources lived on premises. As applications moved to SaaS and devices moved everywhere, the model started to fail.

VPN concentrators became single points of failure and prime targets for attackers. The 2021 incidents involving Pulse Secure, Fortinet and SonicWall illustrated the pattern: a vulnerability in the VPN gateway granted persistent network access. Once inside, attackers moved laterally with little friction, because the network had been designed under the assumption that everyone on it could be trusted.

Zero Trust Network Access reframes the problem. Instead of granting network access, ZTNA grants application access on a per-session, per-identity, per-device basis. The user authenticates not to a network but to an identity-aware proxy, which evaluates context — user identity, device posture, location, time — before allowing each connection. Compromise of one application does not give the attacker the network. Compromise of one user account, with strong device controls, does not give an attacker even all of that user's applications.

The migration is harder than vendors suggest. Legacy applications often depend on protocols that ZTNA cannot proxy easily. Some teams say they wish they had migrated incrementally, starting with the most accessed applications, rather than attempting a flag-day cutover. Others wish vendors would be more honest about which workloads they cannot support. The lesson, repeated across many incidents, is that ZTNA is a multi-year programme rather than a product purchase.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture (nist.gov, PD), CISA advisories on Pulse Secure / Fortinet (cisa.gov, PD).

lesson_notes.warmup: SIEM is the central nervous system of a SOC. The platform itself is rarely the bottleneck — it is the quality of detections, the shape of the data, and the discipline of tuning. A great SIEM with bad logs is just an expensive index.

grammar.title: Passive reporting structures
grammar.rule: Use 'It is said / believed / reported / argued that …' to attribute claims without naming a source. Use 'X is said to + infinitive' as an alternative.

head: Pattern
Example
rows: It is said that mature teams correlate logs across domains.
Mature teams are said to correlate across domains.
It has been observed that detection coverage drives MTTR.
KQL is widely regarded as the most expressive query language.

examples.en: It has been argued that signature-based detection is dead.
Sigma rules are widely regarded as portable detections.

mistakes.wrong: It is said the detection is dead.
mistakes.right: It is said that detection is dead.

mistakes.wrong: Mature teams are said correlate logs.
mistakes.right: Mature teams are said to correlate logs.

lesson_notes.tip: Detection coverage matters more than alert volume. A SOC with 1000 alerts but 80% MITRE ATT&CK coverage is healthier than one with 100 alerts and 20% coverage.

passage.title: What a modern SIEM actually does
passage.text:
Security Information and Event Management platforms collect, normalise and correlate log data from across the enterprise. Endpoint events, network flows, identity logs, cloud audit trails — all feed into a single index that analysts query during incidents and that detection rules monitor continuously. Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel and Elastic dominate the market; the architectural shape they share is more important than the brand differences.

It is widely accepted that data quality determines outcomes. A SIEM ingesting Sysmon with command-line logging will detect process injection that a SIEM with default Windows logs simply cannot see. Log retention also matters. Mature teams retain at least 90 days of detailed events on hot storage and a year on cold storage; cheaper plans that retain only 30 days routinely miss campaigns that begin slowly.

Detection engineering is the practice that turns raw events into actionable signals. Engineers write rules in the platform's query language — SPL for Splunk, KQL for Sentinel, EQL for Elastic. Sigma is the open-source format intended to make rules portable across platforms; it is said to have improved sharing across the community more than any vendor product. The MITRE ATT&CK framework provides the canonical taxonomy that detections map onto.

The most common failure mode of mature SIEMs is alert fatigue. Teams configure broad rules, get flooded with low-fidelity alerts, and develop the habit of closing them quickly. Real attacks then hide in the noise. The discipline that distinguishes effective programmes is alert tuning: understanding why each rule fires, suppressing benign sources, and continuously promoting and demoting detections based on real outcomes. It has been observed that this discipline, more than any technology choice, separates SOCs that detect attacks from SOCs that merely respond to them.
passage.source: Adapted from SANS SOC Survey (sans.org), MITRE ATT&CK (attack.mitre.org), Sigma open-source rules (github.com/SigmaHQ).

exercises.q: Which open format is intended for portable detections?
Default Windows logs are sufficient to detect process injection.
exercises.explain: 'A SIEM with default Windows logs simply cannot see.'

exercises.q: Mature teams retain at least ___ days of detailed events on hot storage.
What does 'alert fatigue' mean and why is it dangerous?
exercises.model: Model: Alert fatigue is when analysts close too many alerts quickly because of overwhelming volume. It is dangerous because real attacks hide in the noise — analysts develop habits of dismissing alerts without proper investigation.

grammar.rule: Have / get + object + past participle = arrange for someone to do it. 'I had the pentest done' = I arranged for someone to test. GET is more informal.

lesson_notes.tip: Always insist on retesting after fixes. A finding marked 'closed' without retest is a finding that will reappear in the next test, often with the same root cause.

passage.title: How a pentest actually works
passage.text:
A penetration test is structured offensive security with permission. The customer commissions a test, defines scope and rules of engagement, and gives the testers controlled authorisation to attack specific systems. The deliverable is a report that translates technical findings into business impact and prioritised remediation.

The phases are well established. Reconnaissance gathers public information: domains, employees, technology stacks, code repositories. Scanning enumerates services, fingerprints versions and identifies known vulnerabilities. Exploitation attempts to gain access in controlled ways. Post-exploitation explores how far an initial foothold can extend: privilege escalation, lateral movement, access to sensitive data. Reporting concludes the engagement with a prioritised list of findings, reproduction steps, and remediation guidance.

The quality of a test depends on more than technique. Mature teams have their assumptions challenged by an external party — assumptions about which systems are exposed, which credentials still work, which paths an attacker would actually take. The findings that surprise customers most are usually not new vulnerabilities but old ones whose impact had been underestimated.

Good organisations do not stop at having the app pentested. They have findings retested after remediation, often by the same team to ensure continuity. They have their cloud configurations independently reviewed. They have credentials harvested in past breaches checked against current accounts. The cycle is continuous, not annual.

Legal context shapes everything. The 2022 update of the US DoJ's Computer Fraud and Abuse Act charging policy declined to prosecute good-faith security research. The UK's Computer Misuse Act of 1990 has not been comparably reformed, and Peruvian Law 30096 follows the older, more restrictive pattern. International testers have to navigate this patchwork carefully when scope crosses borders.
passage.source: Adapted from NIST SP 800-115 (nist.gov, PD), OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (owasp.org, CC BY-SA), US DoJ CFAA charging policy 2022 (justice.gov, PD).

exercises.q: What does a good pentest report translate into?
exercises.explain: 'Translates technical findings into business impact and prioritised remediation.'

exercises.q: Mature organisations only commission an annual pentest.

exercises.model: Model: Without retest, findings closed on paper may not be fixed in practice. Retesting verifies the fix works and ensures the same vulnerability does not reappear in the next engagement with the same root cause.

passage.title: AI on both sides of cybersecurity
passage.text:
AI has changed the offensive landscape faster than the defensive one. Phishing emails generated by large language models pass the linguistic markers that humans used to rely on. Deepfake voice cloning, increasingly realistic with as little as thirty seconds of audio, has been used in CEO-impersonation attacks against finance teams in multiple jurisdictions. The OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Models, first published in 2023 and now in its second iteration, catalogues the new risks: prompt injection, training data poisoning, sensitive information disclosure, model denial of service, supply chain compromise.

Defensive uses are equally significant but often misrepresented. Anomaly detection has been a research topic for decades, with mixed practical results — high false-positive rates have repeatedly turned promising prototypes into shelfware. Recent transformer-based approaches show better generalisation but still require careful baselining and ongoing tuning. The lesson, repeated across many evaluations, is that AI augments analysts rather than replacing them.

The regulatory frame is hardening. The EU AI Act of 2024 imposes obligations proportional to risk, with biometric identification systems and critical-infrastructure AI categorised as high-risk. The US AI Executive Order, the UK AI Safety Institute, and similar bodies in other jurisdictions impose evaluation requirements that are still maturing. Practitioners who recommend that organisations defer their AI security strategy until the regulation settles are increasingly out of step with the threat reality.

For systems engineers the practical answer is to treat AI components like any other untrusted dependency: with strong input validation, output sanitisation, monitoring, and incident response procedures designed for the failure modes of statistical systems.
passage.source: Adapted from OWASP Top 10 for LLMs (owasp.org, CC BY-SA), EU AI Act 2024 public summaries (europa.eu), US AI Executive Order 14110 (whitehouse.gov, PD).

passage.title: What goes wrong in the cloud
passage.text:
The shared-responsibility model is well documented and routinely misunderstood. Major providers publish detailed matrices showing which controls they operate and which the customer must operate. Despite this clarity, the same misconfigurations recur across industries year after year: open storage buckets, over-permissive identity policies, exposed management interfaces, missing logging.

Identity is the new perimeter and the most common point of failure. The 2019 Capital One breach, in which a misconfigured web application firewall allowed an attacker to obtain credentials with excessive privileges, illustrated how a small identity error can produce a large data breach. The phrase 'identity is the new perimeter' has been overused to the point of cliché, but the underlying observation remains valid: in the cloud, what your identities can do matters far more than what your network looks like.

Many of the dominant cloud-security tools — CSPM, CIEM, CNAPP, CWPP — exist precisely to surface these recurring problems. Cloud Security Posture Management continuously scans configurations against best-practice baselines. Cloud Infrastructure Entitlement Management focuses on identity sprawl: the gap between permissions granted and permissions actually used. Cloud-Native Application Protection Platforms attempt to integrate workload protection, posture management and identity governance into a single product, with mixed results in practice.

The consistent message from incident reports is that what matters most is operational discipline. The teams that maintain a clean cloud environment do so through automation: policy as code, infrastructure as code, continuous compliance pipelines. The teams that try to maintain a clean environment through manual review, however well-staffed they are, accumulate drift faster than they can fix it.
passage.source: Adapted from CISA cloud advisories (cisa.gov, PD), AWS Well-Architected Framework Security Pillar (aws.amazon.com), and the Capital One incident analysis (public).

passage.title: DevSecOps in practice
passage.text:
DevSecOps describes the practical integration of security into modern software-delivery pipelines. The conceptual ambition is straightforward: move security feedback close to the developer, where the cost of fixing a vulnerability is lowest. The execution, as ten years of public experience reports show, is harder than the slogan suggests.

Static analysis tools, often grouped under SAST, scan source code for unsafe patterns. The earliest commercial offerings produced more noise than signal; modern open-source tools like Semgrep have shifted the equation by allowing teams to write rules tailored to their codebase. Software Composition Analysis tracks third-party dependencies, comparing them against vulnerability databases and license obligations. The recent SBOM mandates from the US Executive Order 14028 have raised expectations: any product sold to the federal government must now ship with a software bill of materials, listing every dependency by version and identifier.

Dynamic analysis, abbreviated DAST, runs a deployed application and probes it for runtime vulnerabilities. Interactive analysis, IAST, instruments the application to combine the two perspectives. Although DAST has been available for as long as SAST, its integration into pipelines has lagged because dynamic tests take time and require running environments.

Secret scanning is the highest-impact recent addition. Despite the universal advice never to commit secrets to source control, secrets continue to be committed at a steady rate. GitHub's secret-scanning service has prevented millions of credentials from going public; comparable features now exist on every major platform. The lesson, drawn from a decade of incidents, is that automation prevents what training alone cannot.
passage.source: Adapted from US Executive Order 14028 on Cybersecurity (whitehouse.gov, PD), CISA SBOM guidance (cisa.gov, PD), GitHub Security public docs.

passage.title: IoT security at the edge
passage.text:
The number of connected devices on enterprise networks has surpassed the number of managed endpoints in many sectors. Hospitals operate connected medical equipment that runs unsupported operating systems. Manufacturing floors carry industrial controllers designed in an era when network exposure was unthinkable. Retail environments include cameras, point-of-sale terminals, and digital signage from dozens of vendors with widely varying security postures.

The Mirai botnet of 2016 was a wake-up call. Hundreds of thousands of cheap routers and IP cameras were compromised through default credentials and weaponised into the largest DDoS botnet of its era. Subsequent variants — Mozi, Aisuru, Dark Mirai — have continued to demonstrate that the underlying problem is structural rather than incidental.

Regulators have begun to respond. The EU Cyber Resilience Act, adopted in late 2024, imposes baseline security obligations on connected products sold in the EU market. Manufacturers must support products throughout an explicit support window, publish vulnerability disclosure policies, and report exploited vulnerabilities to authorities within tight deadlines. The UK Product Security and Telecommunications Infrastructure Act and the US IoT Cybersecurity Improvement Act follow similar patterns.

For enterprises, the practical defence is segmentation. IoT devices belong on separate VLANs with strict outbound rules. Network detection and response tools that profile devices by behaviour have proven more useful than periodic vulnerability scans, because the devices themselves are often unscannable. Notwithstanding the regulation, default credentials persist in cheap devices for years; treating every IoT endpoint as untrusted by design remains the only durable approach.
passage.source: Adapted from CISA OT advisories (cisa.gov, PD), EU Cyber Resilience Act (europa.eu), Mirai botnet analysis (academic public).

passage.title: Mobile threat models
passage.text:
Modern mobile platforms have stronger default security than any desktop operating system. iOS combines hardware-rooted code signing, application sandboxing, and tightly controlled distribution through the App Store. Android, while more open, has converged on similar architectural protections: SELinux, Verified Boot, Play Protect. Both platforms have largely eliminated the kinds of vulnerabilities that defined desktop malware in the early 2000s.

The attacker's response has been to invest in highly targeted, often state-grade, exploit chains. Mercenary spyware vendors such as NSO Group, Cytrox, and Intellexa have repeatedly demonstrated zero-click iOS chains capable of compromising fully patched devices. The cost of these capabilities is high — millions of dollars per chain — but they are bought and used. Targeted journalists, dissidents, and human-rights defenders have been compromised in dozens of jurisdictions documented by Citizen Lab and Amnesty International.

Mobile Device Management is the enterprise control plane. MDM solutions enrol devices, enforce passcodes, manage app installation, and can wipe corporate data remotely. The friction with personal use is significant; the privacy concerns are legitimate. Modern MDM separates 'work' and 'personal' contexts to reduce the friction, particularly through Android Work Profiles and Apple's User Enrolment.

Under no circumstances should rooted or jailbroken devices be allowed to access corporate resources. Modern attestation primitives — Android Key Attestation, iOS DeviceCheck — let MDM verify device integrity cryptographically, which makes this a technical control rather than a policy hope.
passage.source: Adapted from Citizen Lab public research (citizenlab.ca, CC BY), OWASP Mobile Application Security (owasp.org, CC BY-SA), Apple Platform Security Guide (apple.com).

grammar.title: Modal verbs in legal register
grammar.rule: Legal English uses precise modals: SHALL = obligation, MAY = permission, MUST NOT = prohibition. Misusing them changes legal meaning.

examples.en: The controller shall notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours.

lesson_notes.tip: See the in-text examples above and apply the structure in your Writing draft.

passage.title: GDPR and the global privacy patchwork
passage.text:
The General Data Protection Regulation, in force since May 2018, restructured how organisations process the personal data of EU residents. Six lawful bases authorise processing — consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interests — each with its own evidentiary requirements. The regulation enshrined data subject rights including access, rectification, erasure, portability, and objection, all of which generate operational work for any organisation that holds significant personal data.

Fines have been substantial. Meta, Google, Amazon, and TikTok have each faced multi-hundred-million-euro penalties. The largest fines have been issued for cross-border transfer violations rather than for security incidents, illustrating that compliance with the international transfer rules — Standard Contractual Clauses, transfer impact assessments, the post-Schrems II adequacy framework — has been a primary regulatory focus.

Globally, similar regimes have proliferated. Brazil's LGPD, India's DPDP Act of 2023, China's PIPL, and a growing patchwork of US state laws — California, Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut and counting — each impose different obligations and have different relationships to GDPR. Multinational organisations have to operate compliance programmes that satisfy the strictest applicable rule and document the reasoning when interpretations differ.

For security teams, the practical implications are concrete. Breach notification timelines are tight: 72 hours under GDPR, sometimes shorter under sectoral regimes. Records of processing activities must be current. Data Protection Impact Assessments shall be conducted for high-risk processing. The intersection of legal obligation and engineering reality is permanent and demanding.
passage.source: Adapted from EU Regulation 2016/679 (europa.eu), EDPB guidelines (edpb.europa.eu), and CJEU Schrems II judgment public materials.

lesson_notes.warmup: A great finding badly written gets ignored. A modest finding well written gets prioritised. The hedging language of professional reporting is what makes the difference.

grammar.title: Hedging language
grammar.rule: Hedge claims to signal uncertainty: 'appears to', 'is consistent with', 'with moderate confidence', 'likely / unlikely'. Confident absolutes are reserved for what you can prove.

examples.en: The activity is consistent with early-stage reconnaissance.

lesson_notes.tip: See the in-text examples above and apply the structure in your Writing draft.

passage.title: How a good security report reads
passage.text:
A useful security report is a translation. The technical findings are the source language; business action is the target. Reports that fail this translation generate technical noise without organisational change.

Good reports begin with an executive summary that fits on one page. The summary states the most important finding, its business impact, and the recommended action. Executives who read only the summary should walk away with the right priorities. Detailed technical findings follow, each with a consistent template: description, evidence, impact, likelihood, recommended remediation, and references.

Hedging language is essential. The temptation to overstate findings is real — researchers want their work to be noticed. The cost of overstating is also real: when a finding is described as 'critical' and the customer's investigation finds it to be moderate, all subsequent findings from the same author lose credibility. Mature reporting uses calibrated language: 'with high confidence', 'with moderate confidence', 'consistent with', 'cannot rule out'. The Intelligence Community Directive 203 provides the canonical reference for these distinctions, and increasingly commercial vendors adopt it.

Reproduction steps are non-negotiable. A finding without reproduction steps is closed without verification by sceptical engineers; a finding with clear steps is fixed and retested. Screenshots, request payloads, and tool versions all matter. CVSS and CWE references help defenders triage; without them, prioritisation across dozens of findings becomes guesswork.

Finally, the relationship matters. The first report to a new customer sets the tone. Direct, evidence-based, calibrated language builds credibility that survives the inevitable disagreements about specific findings.
passage.source: Adapted from US ODNI ICD 203 Analytic Standards (odni.gov, PD), MITRE CWE (cwe.mitre.org), FIRST CVSS specifications (first.org).
